title: Template literals — the modern way
instruction:
Backtick strings are the gold standard for building strings in modern JavaScript. They support:

1. **Interpolation** — `${expression}` embeds any expression
2. **Multi-line** — no `\n` needed
3. **Expressions** — math, method calls, ternaries — all valid

Run this. Notice that expressions inside `${}` can be anything.
successMessage: Ternary inside a template literal — ${passed ? "PASS" : "FAIL"} — clean and readable. Template literals replace ugly string concatenation with clarity.

title: length and indexing
instruction:
Strings have a `length` property and you can access characters by index (0-based):

- `str[0]` — first character
- `str[str.length - 1]` — last character
- `str.at(-1)` — last character (modern, cleaner)

Print the first character, last character, and length of each word.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: .at(-1) is the modern way — cleaner than [length-1]. Negative indexes count from the end. .at() works on arrays too.

title: toUpperCase, toLowerCase, trim
instruction:
Three methods you will use constantly:

- `.toUpperCase()` / `.toLowerCase()` — case conversion
- `.trim()` — remove whitespace from both ends
- `.trimStart()` / `.trimEnd()` — one side only

Fix the messy user input by normalising it.
successMessage: trim + toLowerCase is the standard input normalisation pattern. Before comparing usernames, emails, or search queries, always normalise them — otherwise 'Alex' and 'alex' look different.

title: includes, startsWith, endsWith
instruction:
Search inside strings:

- `.includes(substr)` — is this substring anywhere?
- `.startsWith(prefix)` — does it begin with this?
- `.endsWith(suffix)` — does it end with this?

All return `true` or `false`. Use them to validate URLs and emails.
successMessage: Three clean boolean methods — no regex needed for common checks. includes, startsWith, endsWith read almost like English. That's intentional.

title: slice — extract a portion
instruction:
`.slice(start, end)` extracts a substring:

- `str.slice(0, 5)` — first 5 characters
- `str.slice(-3)` — last 3 characters
- `str.slice(7)` — from position 7 to the end

Extract the protocol, domain, and file extension from the URL.
successMessage: slice with negative indexes counts from the end — filename.slice(-4) always gets the last 4 characters regardless of filename length. This is how file extension detection works.

title: split and join
instruction:
`.split(separator)` breaks a string into an array.
`.join(separator)` joins an array back into a string.

They are inverses — split apart, join together. Perfect for CSV parsing, URL building, and formatting.
expectedOutput: Alice and Bob and Carol and David
successMessage: split/join are data transformation workhorses. CSV parsing, URL building, sentence processing, name formatting — all use this pair constantly.

title: replace and replaceAll
instruction:
`.replace(search, replacement)` replaces the **first** match.
`.replaceAll(search, replacement)` replaces **all** matches.

Both accept strings or regular expressions.
expectedOutput: JS is hard. JavaScript takes time. JavaScript is worth it.
JS is hard. JS takes time. JS is worth it.
successMessage: replaceAll is what you almost always want — replace just looks for the first one. The trim + replaceAll pattern cleans up messy user input effectively.

title: padStart, padEnd, repeat
instruction:
String padding and repetition — used for formatting aligned output:

- `str.padStart(width, char)` — pad on the left to reach width
- `str.padEnd(width, char)` — pad on the right
- `str.repeat(n)` — repeat the string n times

Build an aligned receipt using padStart.
successMessage: Aligned columns with padStart and padEnd. Receipts, reports, tables, CLI output — this is how they are formatted. toFixed(2) for prices, padStart for right-alignment.

title: indexOf and search
instruction:
Find the position of something in a string:

- `.indexOf(substr)` — position of first match (-1 if not found)
- `.lastIndexOf(substr)` — position of last match

Build a function that checks if an email is valid — it must contain exactly one '@' and a '.' after the '@'.
hint: email.indexOf('@') must not be -1, email.lastIndexOf('.') must be after the @
successMessage: indexOf returns -1 when nothing is found — checking for > 0 (not just >= 0) ensures the @ isn't the first character. Real email validation uses regex, but this covers the basics.

title: Build a string formatter
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Write a `formatUser(user)` function that takes `{ name, email, score }` and returns a formatted string like:
```
Name:  Alex Rivera
Email: alex@example.com
Score: 87/100 ★★★★
```

Requirements:
- Left-align labels, right-align values
- Score shows star rating: 5 stars for 100, round to nearest star
- Email should be lowercased
hint: Use padEnd for labels, toFixed for score, '★'.repeat(Math.round(score/20)) for stars.

codeChecks.message: Use padEnd or padStart for alignment.
Use repeat() for the star rating.

successMessage: A formatted user card — padEnd, toLowerCase, repeat, toFixed, all working together. String formatting is everywhere: emails, invoices, CLI tools, logs. Chapter 4 done!
